quotedTweet.text:
🧃 Introducing stereOS: a Linux based operating system hardened and purpose built for AI agents.

It's clear that agents need an ACTUAL operating system (not what people are calling an "OS") to witness the full breadth and depth of their capabilities while mitigating the blast radius of autonomous, untrusted actors.

But there are so many problems with AI sandboxes today:

* Going out to the apple store and buying a mac mini will never scale and is way too expensive (obviously)
* Running in Docker is too restrictive (agents can't stand up their own container infrastructure, no sub virtualization, docker-in-docker is very broken)
* Firecracker strips all the hardware so GPU PCIe passthrough, secure boot, FIPs, etc. is out of the question.
* Native VMs are too fat and the overhead of 1 agent per VM is too much.

stereOS takes a different approach: it's a full NixOS system that you boot and then kick off agent sandboxes inside with gVisor + /nix/store namespace mounting. Each agent gets their own kernel and the /nix/store is read only by nature. Even if the agent was somehow able to escape the gVisor virtual kernel, they'd land on the NixOS system as the "agent" user! Not your actual hardware!!

If you want to take a defense-in-depth approach, we support "native" agents that run at the system level kicked off by our `agentd` utility. These agents, on their own, can manage and kick off other sub agents using the internal sandboxing mechanisms.

Today, we're open sourcing all of this:

* stereOS: our purpose built Linux OS - https://t.co/y3BkgdR4m6
* masterblaster: client utility to launch, manage, and orchestrate agents - https://t.co/445Wzlcre1
* stereosd: the stereOS system control plane daemon - https://t.co/uYcecQHIGe
* agentd: the stereOS system agent management daemon - https://t.co/icwQoeKurm

Give it a try, throw us a star, and let me know what you think 🧃⭐️

If you still think DevOps in 2026 is just setting up a CI/CD pipeline like we used to in 2015, you're honestly living in the past. Earlier it was mostly build, test, deploy and done. Now it's completely different.

You're expected to understand the cloud bill, security issues, scaling problems, monitoring gaps, why latency suddenly spiked, and what went wrong during the last incident. Its not just pushing code anymore.

You deploy it, you monitor it, and if it breaks, you're on the call. More tools, yes. But more ownership also.

Why I use edge functions instead of a traditional server:

I have zero servers.
No Express. No Nginx. No Docker. No AWS bill.
Just 10 Supabase edge functions that handle my app.

- Each one deploys in seconds. 
- Scales to zero when nobody's using it. 
- Costs nothing until it runs.
- A traditional server would be running 24/7 for our 150 users.
- Edge functions run for 200ms when someone taps a button.

That's the difference between burning money and spending it.

@martin_casado well there goes my weekend 😅. Thank you for the artisnal code work!

Next week I'm adding OpenClaw to my stack. Here's why.

I've been vibe coding production apps for the past year. 

Internal tools, AI-powered customer tools, full web apps. All built with Cursor + Claude. All shipped and in use.

But there's a gap in the workflow.

Cursor + Claude is incredible for building. You describe what you want, it writes the code, you ship. 

But once the app is live, the ops side still runs on manual effort. 

Feature requests come in through email. Bug reports live in Slack threads. Workflows depend on me being at my laptop.

OpenClaw fills that gap.

The mental model I'm working with:

Cursor builds the product.
OpenClaw runs the product workflows.
Telegram becomes the remote control.

So next week I'm setting up my first flow:

Telegram message > OpenClaw > GitHub Issue > Cursor implements > deploy

The idea is simple. Text a feature request or bug report from my phone. OpenClaw formats it into a proper spec with acceptance criteria. Pushes it to GitHub. Then I open Cursor and tell Claude to implement it.

No context switching. No lost requests. No waiting until I'm back at my desk.

I'll be documenting the entire setup, what works, what breaks, and what's actually useful vs. what's just hype.

If you're building with AI tools or curious about how these pieces fit together, follow along. I'll share everything here.

Anyone already running OpenClaw in their workflow? What's your setup look like?

You're letting AI deploy to production?"

No. AI generates the code, you review it, you push it. Same workflow as any other code change. Encore's primitives just make the generated code safe by default.

@AnthropicAI @claudeai Built with Claude code by @bcherny. I'm not a specialist in any one stack. I'm the person who learns whatever the problem requires and ships it. 20 years of doing that without a degree. AI just removed the last barrier between "generalist" and "production-grade."

If you run:

On a table with 10M rows…

You just created a production incident.

• Network payload control
• Stable response time
• Scalable APIs

The most important line in deployment strategy isn't about rolling updates, canary, or blue/green.

It's this: decouple "deploying" code from "releasing" to users.

Deploy whenever you want. Release when you're ready. Feature flags make this possible without a single new deployment.

Vibe coding everywhere these days

But vibes crash without structure

Layer it with C4 Model:

1. Context - system overview
2. Containers - apps, DBs, services
3. Components - modules inside
4. Code - classes & functions

Prompt your AI agent with this - ships faster, scales better

C4 or something else for arch diagrams?

3/5
Checkpoint position is not committed state.

That gap is where production incidents show up:

- duplicate writes
- inconsistent records
- audit confusion
- painful rollback decisions

This is a systems boundary issue, not a model quality issue.

MTTR (Mean Time To Resolution) is the average time to fully resolve an incident—from detection or report until it's completely fixed and verified.

Formula: Total time to resolve all incidents ÷ Number of incidents.

Key metric in IT, DevOps, and SRE for tracking operational efficiency. Got a specific context or calculation in mind?

openclaw often makes mistakes when modifying configuration files by itself, leading to service interruptions.

▪️ Custom Load Balancer Instead of just dropping in Nginx, I wrote a custom Load Balancer in Go. It implements a Round-Robin algorithm to distribute traffic across my worker nodes, simulating how a production-grade reverse proxy manages horizontal scaling.

@supabase This is completely unacceptable. Our production applications that rely on Supabase APIs and databases are fully down — real users cannot log in, and our business is taking a direct hit because of this.

Suggesting a VPN or alternate DNS as a "workaround" is not a solution for production-grade infrastructure. Our end users cannot be expected to configure VPNs just to access our apps.

We need answers to the following:
• A clear ETA for full resolution
• Details on what exactly caused this
• Compensation or SLA credits for the downtime

Also, some critical questions once restoration is complete:
• Will login & sign-in functionality automatically resume for our apps, or do we need to make changes on our end?
• Will our existing projects that use Supabase APIs & databases continue to work normally after restoration, or will there be data/config issues we need to fix?

We trusted Supabase as a reliable backend — please act with the urgency this situation demands and give us a clear, transparent update.

@levelsio Always worth adding a CHECK constraint or an upper bound on currency columns. Nobody thinks the Iranian Rial will hit 1,000,000 per dollar until it does. One line in your schema saves a production incident.

Sequential Cypher pipeline with fail-fast Guardian validators is production-sharp—early abort saves cycles and keeps every mutation atomic. 

xAI runs ordered validation stages on KG writes: sequential checks inside one tx, rollback on first violation. Batching all checks would hide failures and spike latency on bad data.

Sunday deploy ritual: pushed updates to all 6 agents on the Mac mini. Total downtime: 0 seconds.

The stack: OpenClaw gateway, 6 specialized agents (ops, research, code, growth), markdown memory, 14 repos under management.

No Kubernetes. No Docker. No cloud bill. Just a $600 Mac mini and plain text files that agents actually read.

Most "AI infrastructure" is a solution looking for a problem. A good https://t.co/SIctpI6cNC file beats a $50K/yr platform.

API exposing: No auth on public APIs means anyone (no login needed) can query your endpoints and pull private user data.

Surprise cloud bill: No rate limits let scrapers/bots hammer your API nonstop, burning through server resources and racking up huge unexpected costs.

3. Open CORS (*): Any website can call your APIs from a browser, silently stealing data via cross-origin requests.

Vibe-code fast, but lock these down before launch!

Hobby projects break on a 429 error. Production systems move to the next model in line.

If our primary provider returns a 503 error, the system retries with a secondary provider instantly. The user never sees a glitch. This is how you build trust.

Code is cheap.
Infrastructure isn’t.

“Just build it” is what people say when they’ve never paid an AWS bill.
